sentences.text: Henri Chaim Wittelsohn.
H-E-N-R-I C-H-A-I-M W-I-T-T-E-L-S-O-H-N.
Was Chaim Wittelsohn.
My hidden name was Andre Barniche.
A-N-D-R-E B-A-R-N-I-C-H-E.
Many through life.
I was called [?
Han, ?] Hank, Frenchy.
But that happened much later on in life.
I was born September 25th, 1935.
I just celebrated my 60th birthday.
Born in Brussels, Belgium.
My father's name was Szmul Jacob Vitelson.
And my mother was [?
Fajga ?] [?
Leah ?] Vitelson.
It was then spelled a V-I-T-E-L-S-O-N.
Yes, I did.
My [SIGHS] oldest brother and sister, who were twins, his name was-- the name that I can give you is what I called him, what we called him by, which was Mendle.
That was my brother.
My sister was Anna.
They were twins.
I then had another sister called [?
Mindla. ?] She was born, I believe, Mirjam.
Then another brother, Vigdor, another sister, by the name of Fajga, or Fella.
Yiddish.
My parents spoke Yiddish to us, and we spoke back to them in Yiddish.
Between us, the brothers and sisters, I believe we spoke a lot of French also, which was the native language of Belgium.

[SIGHS] As far as I can remember, we've had a textile factory-- if you want to call it that, since it was the cellar of our home-- where they were manufacturing knit goods, which they put together into sweaters.
And we had a retail store upstairs.
And they sold the goods that they manufactured downstairs upstairs in the store.
My mother also had, before that, I believe, a side job where she worked in a rubber factory.
And she did that for a lot of years, and a lot of long hours.
It was during, and I think previous to the-- to having that textile factory and store.
My earliest memory in Belgium.
Well, we were a very happy family, I remember.
I was the youngest of all, and certainly I was doted on.
And I remember a lot of love from my sisters and my brothers.
I don't remember too much of that with my mother and my father.
Somehow, they were too busy to kind of raise us-- too busy making a living-- a large family in a strange country, because they weren't born in Belgium.
And so the best that I can remember is still having a happy life, and a very-- being very well loved by my brothers and sisters.
They were both born in Lodz, Poland, and immigrated to Belgium in 1929, I believe.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And the reason for that immigration was pretty much the story of World War II.
They also were escaping the anti-Semitism in Poland, the [? balance ?] of the pogroms, and so on.
So there was a lot of discrimination in Poland.
They thought they would have a better life in-- in Belgium.
So they came to Belgium with then five children.
I wasn't born yet.
And I was then born in Belgium in 1935.
Again, as a child in Brussels, did you celebrate birthdays and Jewish holidays?
What is your memory of that?
My memories are that my father was a cantor in a-- in a shul.
And the best memory I have of my Jewish upbringing was that we spoke Yiddish, of course, in the house, and also that I remember him singing in shul.
I don't remember going there habitually.
I-- I remember high holidays where I sat and listened to him.

But I don't remember a real religious upbringing within our house, although I do know that both my father and mother were religious, and they certainly believed in God, and they certainly went to shul on the holidays.
I don't have any memories of between the holidays-- on Jewish holidays, that is.
Again, the war started [SIGHS] when I was about six years old.
And I do recollect certain dinners around the table with the whole family.
Whether they were for holidays or not, I don't quite recall.
There is no strong memory of celebrating the holidays around the table in the house.
My first recollection of anti-Semitism, and being called a Jew, or not being-- or the other people not wanting to associate with me-- had to do more at the beginning of the invasion by the Germans.
All the friends that I had-- a lot of gentile friends, because we didn't live in a-- really in a Jewish neighborhood.
It was quite mixed.
I do remember at that time that all the friends that I had-- and we played on the streets together-- all of a sudden [BACKGROUND NOISE] didn't want to have any relationships with me.
All of a sudden they didn't want to play with me.
I was kind of-- began to be an outcast.
And I do remember running home one-- one day and asking my sisters and brothers, because they're the ones that I communicated with the most, what that was about.
I didn't do anything.
I didn't understand what was happening.

And they tried to explain to me that being a Jew at that-- at that time was different, and that there was-- as much as they could explain anti-Semitism to me-- that the parents of my friends are probably the ones that were suggesting no association with me at that time, because then the Germans had already come in.
And they began-- And I think that the anti-Semitism that they had, which was, I think, restrained at that point, really came out.
And you saw what their true feelings were.
As I said, my first recollection of anti-Semitism, or coming across that, was my friends no longer wanting to play with me.
Not quite.
It took a lot more than that.
It took many of the things that I finally got to see on the streets of Belgium.
Germans dragging-- well, at the time, of course, we had finally been made to wear the Jewish star.
Which of course made [INAUDIBLE] I started to understand how different we were, that we were made to wear a yellow star with a J on it, and my friends that weren't Jewish did not.
And then I started seeing some arresting and deportation and beating and things of that sort, and all the time asking why.
And every time, it was pounded into me that only because we were Jewish, and we had to be careful.
And we had to be very careful about who we associated with.
And then it started to be explained to me about uniforms and Germans.

And the thing that I thought was a very exciting thing happening in Belgium-- I didn't quite understand the word invasion at the time-- started to hit home, that these people were to be feared, that they didn't like Jews.
At the time it wasn't explained to me exactly what they were doing to Jews.
I don't think that anyone knew.
But we certainly knew that we were different then, and that life was changing.
And this is when I began to understand things were not the same any longer.
Well, it came quite early.

Well, again, to go back to the fact that we were wearing the Jewish star, and the fact that many Jews were-- were taken by force, and beaten on the streets because they were Jews, and my questions were, why were they doing that to those people?
And it was explained in simple terms, that because they were Jews, I started now fearing the fact that this could happen to me.
And so as much as I could, I-- I wore the star, but I hid the star as much as I could hide the star.
So fear began to come, as I remember it.
When I used to be able to walk by German soldiers, all of a sudden, if I could I would have gone across the street rather than pass them near, or being near to them.
And also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I no longer spoke to my mother and father in Yiddish.
I started speaking to them in French.
Those were the first little signs.

The bigger sign, when I knew that things were totally different, is-- and I'm certainly jumping a little bit on that-- but one day I was playing on the street by myself by that time, because no one was playing with me any longer, at least the gentiles weren't.
And then the Jews somehow weren't on the streets anymore.
But I still insisted on playing on the street right in front of the house.
And my father was reading the newspaper in front of the house.
And a man, a civilian man, came on a bicycle, rode by us, pulled out a gun, and shot my father.
And [BACKGROUND NOISE] when my father fell to the ground, this man came back and shot him again and again and again-- if remember, three or four times.
And I saw my father fall to the ground, bleeding, calling for help.
And I went over to him, and I held him in my arms.
And he was never able to tell me why, who, where.
I didn't quite understand at the time.
And he died in my arms.
This is when my sisters and brothers and my mother came running out of the house, and came over to me.
And I was just stunned.
I didn't know what was happening, and-- and why it had happened.
[SIGHS] And when we then had a chance in-- in the house, they explained to me.
Because then I believe he was-- just because he was a Jew.
And-- and I was hoping that really it was the Germans that had shot him, because he was a Jew.
Later on I came to find out differently, but that's a different story that I don't know if you'd like to hear it now or later.
The Germans had already been there for almost two years.
[SIGHS]
In June
What I remember at the time was they said, this is a war.
They tried to explain the war to us-- to me.
And at the time, it was kind of left that he was Jewish, and he was shot by the Germans.
But I couldn't understand why him, and why would they hide in civilian clothes?
I mean, I saw Germans shoot Jews all over the place in-- in daylight.
They weren't hiding.
They were in uniform.
It wasn't a secret.
These things were happening.
So why did this happen?
And I questioned that for a lot of years later.
But at that time, I knew only one thing.
I didn't connect it with that.
But the store and the textile factory that we had was all of a sudden instead of producing sweaters for people, for-- for retail market-- we were just producing hats.
I remember that very distinctly.
And I also remember that those hats were never sold in the store, but every once in a while there was a truck with Germans that came by and picked up these cartons full of hats.
And I don't believe I asked then what that was.
It wasn't-- it wasn't clear to me.
I was just too young to understand any of this connection.

But I later on [SIGHS] asked many questions after the war, and some during the war, and found out that in those days a lot of Jews did what they had to do to survive another day, and to protect their family.
And I came to find out-- and this I believe in very, very strongly today, so I can live with that-- that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] deals were made.
That the Germans used whatever skills they could get out of the Jews at that time, whether they were diamond cutters, or whether they were manufacturers or whatever.
They used their skills for their purposes.
And I came to understand that because he was in textile, and he understood all about sewing and doing and manufacturing clothing, that these hats were necessary.
And I understand that these hats went to the Russian front.
They were warm hats.
They went to Russia front for the Germans.
And that the deal made was you manufactured this, you live one more day, so does your family.
And I'm convinced now, and I have to live with the fact that a lot of people made certain deals, just to survive another day.
But I guess it wasn't understood that way by the underground and the Belgians.
And because all they could see is that he was helping the Germans in some way, he was called a collaborator, and they decided to kill him.
And so now I know that my father was killed by the Belgian underground, possibly even the Jewish Belgian underground.
And [SIGHS] I-- I have mixed feelings about it.
But I have to tell myself that he did the right thing for his family.
Well, things became, at that point, untenable.
And we saw the convoys starting to come, the Germans coming with trucks, rounding up Jews all over the neighborhoods, putting them in trucks, and taking them away.
And we started to hear stories about-- not necessarily yet concentration camps, but the Jews that were taken never came back.
And in those times, we believed that they were taken to work camps and so on.
That's what was explained to us.
And this is what we read, I suppose, in newspapers or heard on radios.
But we-- we knew that we had to get away from there.
So the-- the underground was quite active in those days, and the Jewish underground even more so, in trying to help and hide Jews, and also to get them away from the country.
And this is now things that I understand, my mother had explained to me, because I wasn't quite sure how we wound up on the road to Switzerland.
But whatever the arrangements were, it was arranged that our whole family would go to Switzerland, which was then a neutral country.
I guess it still was.
[LAUGHS] And there we would be saved.
So we packed up whatever we could.
[SIGHS] I do remember that a-- a van came to pick us up.
And we packed up all our belongings, whatever we could put together and carry ourselves.
And we were on our way to Switzerland.
Now, I don't recall today whether it was the French border or the Swiss border.
But at that border-- and we did have papers that were falsified, I'm sure, by the underground and all of those things-- we were arrested at the border.
And we were arrested by the Gestapo who happened to have had our names on a list.
They called us out by names, one by one.
They knew when and where we would be crossing.
And they arrested us and took us back to Malines, which was an assembly-- detention camps-- for the Jews of Belgium.
Well-- [SIGHS] it was 1942, definitely.
Well, this is where a little story, that-- that is still very, very puzzling to me, is happening.
My father died in June 1942.
We know that for a fact.
The convoy was after my father died, and I have some documents to show that.
Yet I remember that my father was behind me, protecting me, when we were arrested.
Yet I know today that he had died a couple of months earlier.
I know that for a fact.
So my-- my days and what I remember of this whole situation, that particular item, is-- is really a puzzlement to me.
I could swear to today that my father was there to protect me-- and on our way to Switzerland, we were arrested.
But he was not.
So we were taken to [SIGHS] Malines, where we were-- Could you spell Malines?
M-A-L-I-N-E-S.
In Belgium.
Excuse me.
[DRINKS] There we were-- I remember being hustled into Gestapo headquarters, and-- and an office of a Gestapo officer.
All my brothers and my sister and my mother, we were still together at the time.
And I remember a very stark office-- just one desk in the middle of a room with a Gestapo officer, who-- we were all on one side of a wall facing this officer.
And he was going to question us-- or he did question us-- one by one.
He was very interested in who had organized this-- this-- this escape and who had done the paperwork, and who these people were, and where they were and so on.
So one by one, we were questioned and then searched.
But something, if anything can be funny about the war, happened.
It was very funny, and saved our live later on.

Is that all my brothers and sisters were all together next to each other on one side of the wall, and the person that was to search us for whatever we had-- because people were known, or Jews were known to have taken all their belongings, and whatever money they may have put together, that they came to Switzerland with them.
And at the time, they thought, my family thought, that the safest person to carry this would be me.
I was just a child, so it was put into kind of a false seam in a-- in a shirt that I was wearing.
So we had this money, which was gold coins, because Belgian money didn't mean anything at that point anymore.
And a lot of Jews were switching over their monies to gold coins, which could be exchanged anywhere, I guess.
And this was sewn in-- in my shirt.
But what happened is they, for some reason, questioned the older brothers and sister first.
I guess they didn't think that anything was happening with me.
And every time they questioned and searched a brother or a sister, they were sent back and put back next to us, next to the-- next to my brothers and sister who hadn't been searched.
So I do remember my brother being searched, coming back into the room, standing next to me.
And my mother went-- was the next person to be interrogated by this Gestapo officer.
She went right in front of him in the line-- in his line of vision.

And while she was talking to him-- I'll never forget that-- she made a gesture like this, which was meant for my brother to take whatever I was hiding, and take it and put it on himself, since he had been searched.
And that's exactly what he did.
While this was happening and the officer was very, very involved in questioning of my mother, he went into the back and pulled out the tubes.
It wasn't-- not plastic at the time, but whatever it was in, the gold coins.
And he took it and put it in his pockets, since he had been searched.
And that, later on, will show where it did save my mother, and my sister, and my life later on.
But this is what happened in Malines.
And after interrogation we were put into detention cells with a lot of other Jews at the time.
That's exactly right, yes.
Then one by one, the rest of our family.
And I was last being searched.
And of course, there was nothing we were hiding at the time.
But the money was quite safe.
Then something that I-- I still have a problem finding out about happened.

I don't know whether my mother was able to make some kind of an arrangement or, as I understand it today, the Belgian queen at the time had appealed to the Germans in 1942-- it was still possible to do that-- for the-- for the saving of the children, to-- to not to be taken to concentration camps and so on.
Or there was some kind of arrangement with somebody.
But all I know is that my brother, my younger-- not my younger brother.
I'm the youngest.
But the youngest of the brother before me, a sister who was a little bit older than I was, and myself were to be released.
And my two-- my one brother and two sisters were to be taken at that time, they said, to work camps.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my mother was to be taken.
And I remember her pleading, saying that-- that she had no one.
No one was there to take care of the children.
Where would they go?
What would they do?
And whatever the situation was-- and I guess I'll never know, because nobody's alive today to be able to explain to me-- she was released with us.
So we were released from Malines.
One brother, at that time, and two sisters were taken to concentration camps.
And I now have proof that-- well, we know they never came back.
The chronology was that we had to come back to Brussels from Malines.
And we hoped and then thought that we had a place to come back to, our home, the one that we had left.
Still 1942.
And I would believe that, since they were taken by convoy in October of 1942, it must have been the month of October.
Maybe somewhere in that particular area.
And I remember coming back to the house that we had escaped from in Belgium, and thought that we could just walk into our home.
Now, another lesson for me was that I knew there was anti-Semitism in Belgium long before the Germans invaded.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] But I didn't know to what extreme and what extent.
And in that house, where we had the store and the factory, we also had tenants-- gentile Belgian tenants-- living upstairs.

And when we came back to the house, those gentiles, Belgian tenants, came down and said this was no longer our home, that they had taken over, that we were just dirty Jews, and they didn't care what happened to us, but we were not coming into the house any longer.
And of course, we were shocked, surprised.

And my brother, who was, I believe, about 10 years older, 8 years older than I was, and somewhat of a-- [LAUGHS] a short fuse hothead, who believed that this was completely outrageous, had an altercation with the people on the street, in the doorway, wanting to go into the house.
And I remember my sister and myself trying to help him, because there was a-- a fight ensued.
And somebody called the police.
And the police in those days, at least some of them, most of them really, were working hand in hand with the Gestapo.
And they arrested my brother.
He was turned over to the Gestapo, and then also was taken back to Malines, and to a concentration camp.
And I never heard from him again.
So the only three that were left was my mother, my older sister-- she was six years older than I was-- and myself.
And I was at that time about seven or eight, I believe.
And--
